revision.text:
[[ar:أسورا (بوذية)]][[bg:Асура (будизъм)]][[bn:অসুর (বৌদ্ধধর্ম)]][[de:Asura (Buddhismus)]][[en:Asura (Buddhism)]][[es:Asura (budismo)]][[hu:Aszúra (buddhizmus)]][[ja:阿修羅]][[ko:아수라 (불교)]][[my:အသူရကာယ်]][[pt:Asura (budismo)]][[sl:Asure (budizem)]][[ta:அசுரர் (பௌத்தம்)]][[tyv:Азарлар]][[wuu:阿修罗（佛教）]][[zh:阿修罗 (佛教)]] {{Short description|Demigod in Buddhism|qq=}} {{other uses|Asura (disambiguation)|qq=}} [[File:Asura Dvarapala Borobudur.jpg|thumb|300px|The guarding figure asura giant ''{{ill|Dvarapala|en|Dvarapala|qid=Q919849|lt=Dvarapala|1=Dvarapala}}'' holding mace flanked by two {{ill|Apsara|en|Apsara|qid=Q148773|lt=Apsara|1=Apsara}}s. The bas-relief of lower outer wall of {{ill|Borobudur|en|Borobudur|qid=Q42798|lt=Borobudur|1=Borobudur}} separating Kamadhatu and Rupadhatu realm. 8th century Central Java, Indonesia.]] {{Infobox Buddhist term | title = Asura | pi = असुर | sa = असुर | bn = অসুর | my = အသုရာနတ် | zh = 阿修羅 | zh-Latn = Āxiūluó | ja = {{ruby|阿|あ|qq=}}{{ruby|修|しゅ|qq=}}{{ruby|羅|ら|qq=}} | ja-Latn = ashura | km = អសុរ | km-Latn = Asorak | ko = 아수라 | ko-Latn = asura | lo = | mnw = | mnw-Latn = | shn = | shn-Latn = | si = | si-Latn = | ta = அசுரன் | tl = assura | th = อสูร | bo = ལྷ་མ་ཡིན་ | bo-Latn = lha.ma.yin | vi = A Tu La | as =অসুৰ | as-Latn = Oxur | id = }} {{Buddhism|qq=}} An '''asura''' ({{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} and {{ill|Pali|en|Pali|qid=Q36727|lt=Pali|1=Pali}}: असुर) in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} is a {{ill|Demigod|en|Demigod|qid=Q466470|lt=Demigod|1=Demigod}} or [[:en:Titan (mythology)|titan]]Robert Beer The ''Handbook of Tibetan Buddhist Symbols'' Serindia Publications 2003 {{ISBN|978-1-932-47603-3|qq=}} page 246 of the {{ill|Desire realm|en|Desire realm|qid=Q4447630|lt=Kāmadhātu|1=Desire realm}}.{{cite book |title=The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism |year=2013 |author=Robert E. Buswell Jr |author2=Donald S. Lopez Jr |isbn=978-1-4008-4805-8 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DXN2AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA411 |page=411|qq=}} They are described as having three heads with three faces each and either four or six arms.{{cite book |title=Indian Influence on the Art of Japan |year=2010 |author= Sampa Biswas |isbn=978-81-7211-269-1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fLjcMfMGP10C&pg=PA72 |page=72|qq=}} ==Origins and etymology== The Buddhist ''asuras'' have a few myths distinctive from the ''[[:en:Asura (Hinduism)|asura]]s'' of Hinduism, which are only found in Buddhist texts. In its Buddhist context, the word is sometimes translated "[[:en:Titan (mythology)|titan]]", "{{ill|Demigod|en|Demigod|qid=Q466470|lt=Demigod|1=Demigod}}", or "".Robert Beer The ''Handbook of Tibetan Buddhist Symbols'' Serindia Publications 2003 {{ISBN|978-1-932-47603-3|qq=}} page 246 Buddhaghosa explains that their name derives from the myth of their defeat at the hands of the god {{ill|Śakra (Buddhism)|en|Śakra (Buddhism)|qid=Q1444745|lt=Śakra|1=Śakra (Buddhism)}}. According to the story, the asuras were dispossessed of their state in {{ill|Trāyastriṃśa|en|Trāyastriṃśa|qid=Q1360289|lt=Trāyastriṃśa|1=Trāyastriṃśa}} because they became drunk and were thrown down [[:en:Mount Sumeru|Mount Sumeru]]. After this incident, they vowed never to drink {{ill|sura (alcoholic drink)|en|sura (alcoholic drink)|qid=Q4446400|lt=sura|1=Sura (alcoholic drink)}} again. ==Character== While all the gods of the Kāmadhātu are subject to passions to some degree, the asuras above all are addicted to them, especially wrath, pride, envy, insincerity, falseness, boasting, and bellicosity. The Great Calm-Observation by {{ill|Zhiyi|en|Zhiyi|qid=Q197778|lt=Zhiyi|1=Zhiyi}} says:
Always desiring to be superior to others, having no patience for inferiors and belittling strangers; like a hawk, flying high above and looking down on others, and yet outwardly displaying justice, worship, wisdom, and faith — this is raising up the lowest order of good and walking the way of the Asuras.
The asuras are said to experience a much more pleasurable life than humans, but they are plagued by envy for the devas, whom they can see just as animals perceive humans.{{Citation needed|date=January 2019|qq=}} The asuras of some inferior realms however, are malevolent (such as the corruptor Mara) and can be referred to as demons.{{Citation needed|date=July 2023|qq=}} They are alternatively called ''{{ill|Rakshasa|en|Rakshasa|qid=Q1049467|lt=Rakshasa|1=Rakshasa}}s''. They are sometimes referred to as ''pūrvadeva'' (Pāli: ''pubbadeva''), meaning "ancient gods."{{cite book |last1=Malalasekera |first1=G.P. |title=Dictionary of Pali Proper Names |date=2007 |publisher=Motilal Banarsidass Publishers |isbn=978-81-208-3020-2 |url=http://www.aimwell.org/DPPN/asura.html |chapter=Asurā|qq=}} ==Deva-Asura War== The Asuras formerly lived in the {{ill|Trāyastriṃśa|en|Trāyastriṃśa|qid=Q1360289|lt=Trāyastriṃśa|1=Trāyastriṃśa}} world on the peak of [[:en:Sumeru|Sumeru]] with the other gods of that world. When {{ill|Śakra (Buddhism)|en|Śakra (Buddhism)|qid=Q1444745|lt=Śakra|1=Śakra (Buddhism)}} became the ruler of that world, the asuras celebrated by drinking a lot of Gandapāna wine, a liquor so strong that Śakra forbade the other gods to drink it. Weakened by their drunkenness, the asuras could not resist when Śakra had the whole lot of them thrown over the edge of Trāyastriṃśa into what would become the Asura-world at the base of Sumeru. A tree grows there called Cittapātali; when the asuras saw it blossom, they saw that it was different from the Pāricchattaka (Sanskrit: Pāriyātra) tree which had grown in their old home, and they knew that they were dispossessed. They now meditated on war. In armor and weapons, they climbed up the steep slopes of Sumeru "like ants." Śakra set out to meet them, but was forced to retreat because of their numbers. Passing through the forest where the {{ill|Garuda|en|Garuda|qid=Q188676|lt=garuḍas|1=Garuda}} live on his flying chariot, Śakra saw that his passage was destroying the nests of the garuḍas and ordered his charioteer Mātali to turn back. When the pursuing asuras saw Śakra turn about, they felt certain that he must be coming back with an even larger army, and they fled, ceding all the ground they had gained.{{cite book |last1=Chalmers |first1=Robert |title=The Jataka Volume I |date=1895 |url=https://www.sacred-texts.com/bud/j1/j1034.htm |access-date=2019-11-06 |chapter=No. 31. Kulāvaka-Jātaka|qq=}} Despite their many wars, there was eventually a partial concord between the Trāyastriṃśa gods and the asuras. This came about because Śakra fell in love with Sujā (also known as [[:en:Shachi|Shachi]]), daughter of the Asura chief {{ill|Vemacitrin|en|Vemacitrin|qid=Q3538740|lt=Vemacitrin|1=Vemacitrin}}. Vemacitrin had given Sujā the right to choose her own husband at an assembly of the Asuras, and she chose Śakra, who had attended disguised as an aged Asura. Vemacitrin thus became Śakra's father-in-law. ==Happiness== [[File:観音と阿修羅_Avalokitasvara_and_Asura.jpg|thumb|An illustration from an 1866 Japanese book. Asura, who is described as an incarnation of [[Kannon|Bodhisattva Kannon]] in this scene, gives a Buddhism sermon to folks.]] The asura realm is one of the realms one can be reborn into within the [[Saṃsāra_(Buddhism)#Realms_of_rebirth|six realms]]. Rebirth here is a result of experiencing the {{ill|Phala|en|Phala|qid=Q7180216|lt=fruits|1=Phala}} of wholesome karma while engaging in unwholesome karma. The placement of the asura realm in {{ill|Buddhist cosmology|en|Buddhist cosmology|qid=Q1308653|lt=Buddhist cosmology|1=Buddhist cosmology}} varies among traditions. Sometimes the asura realm is recognized as one of happiness, existing beneath the worlds of the devas and humans. In other schemes, it is viewed as a fourth addition to the usual three evil paths that make up the animal realm, ghost realm and hell realm. In schools that recognize the {{ill|Desire realm|en|Desire realm|qid=Q4447630|lt=Desire realm|1=Desire realm}} as consisting of five realms, the asura realm tends to be included among the deva realm. In {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|en|Tibetan Buddhism|qid=Q483889|lt=Tibetan Buddhism|1=Tibetan Buddhism}}, the addition of the asuras in the six-world {{ill|Bhavacakra|en|Bhavacakra|qid=Q1340222|lt=Bhavacakra|1=Bhavacakra}} was created in Tibet at the authority of {{ill|Je Tsongkhapa|en|Je Tsongkhapa|qid=Q323439|lt=Je Tsongkhapa|1=Je Tsongkhapa}}. The [[:en:Ekottara Āgama|Ekottara Āgama]] and the [[Saddharmasmṛtyupasthāna Sūtra]] explain that asuras are divided among the realms of ghosts and animals. In the former case, they are powerful, high-Ranking demons reminiscent of gods such as {{ill|Mara (demon)|en|Mara (demon)|qid=Q1352021|lt=Māra|1=Mara (demon)}}. In the latter case, they are like fearsome beasts that live 84000 yojanas beneath the ocean floor.The bestial asuras are reminiscent of those gods and creatures of the deep that personify characteristics like chaos and voracity, such as the {{ill|Leviathan|en|Leviathan|qid=Q192677|lt=Leviathan|1=Leviathan}}, {{ill|Fenrir|en|Fenrir|qid=Q182560|lt=Fenrir|1=Fenrir}}, {{ill|Typhon|en|Typhon|qid=Q180237|lt=Typhon|1=Typhon}}, etc. ==Asurendra== The leaders of the asuras are called ''asurendra'' ({{ill|Pali|en|Pali|qid=Q36727|lt=Pāli|1=Pali}}: ''Asurinda'', {{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: 阿修羅王; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Āxiūluó-wáng''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|qid=Q192509|lt=Romaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Ashura-ō''), literally meaning "Asura-lord". There are several of these, as the Asuras are broken into different tribes or factions. Among them are the bow-wielding {{ill|Danava (Hinduism)|en|Danava (Hinduism)|qid=Q1159280|lt=Dānaveghasa|1=Danava (Hinduism)}} Asuras, and the terrible-faced {{ill|Kalakeyas|en|Kalakeyas|qid=Q86735592|lt=Kālakañjakas|1=Kalakeyas}}. [[Image:ASURA detail Kohfukuji.JPG|left|thumb|Asura in [[Kōfuku-ji]], {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, 734, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese.|242x242px]]In Pali texts, names that are found include ''Vepacitti'', ''Rāhu'' (believed to be synonymous with ''Verocana''), ''Pahārāda'', ''Sambara'', ''Bali'', ''Sucitti'', and ''{{ill|Mara (demon)|en|Mara (demon)|qid=Q1352021|lt=Namucī|1=Mara (demon)}}''. According to {{ill|Buddhaghosa|en|Buddhaghosa|qid=Q335247|lt=Buddhaghosa|1=Buddhaghosa}}, the three primary leaders were Vepacitti, Rāhu and Pahārāda.{{cite book |last1=Malalasekera |first1=G.P. |title=Dictionary of Pali Proper Names, Volume 1 |date=2003 |publisher=Asian Educational Services |isbn=978-81-206-1823-7 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=x8ObMQ1GGsUC&q=%22buddhaghosa%22+%22asura%22+%22three%22&pg=PA175|qq=}} Mahayana literature tends to recognize four primary leaders, whose biographies are explained in detail in both the [[Saddharmasmṛtyupasthāna Sūtra]] and the [[Daśabhūmika Sūtra]]. According to this tradition, these asuras live 84,000 [[:en:yojanas|yojanas]] beneath the ocean floor on the northern side of [[:en:Mount Sumeru|Mount Sumeru]], which are divided into four layers. *'''[[:en:Rāhu|Rāhu]]''' **Abode: His body is large like Mount Sumeru and he resides in the "Bright Light Castle" ({{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: 光明城; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Guāngmíng-chéng''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|qid=Q192509|lt=Romaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Kōmyō-jō'') that is 8,000 yojanas in height and width, and 21,000 yojanas beneath the first layer of ocean floor. **Karma: In a past life, he was a [[brahmin]] who managed to save a stupa from being burned down and vowed that in a future life he would procure a higher status. However, he practiced killing and because he did not cultivate all wholesome karmas, his body was destroyed and he fell into the asura realm. **Lifespan: 500 human years is equal to a day and night in Rāhu's realm, which lasts for 5,000 years. *'''[[Mahabali|Bali]]''' or ''Baḍi'' **Bio: His name is Sanskrit for "powerful", which refers to his defeat and binding by {{ill|Śakra (Buddhism)|en|Śakra (Buddhism)|qid=Q1444745|lt=Śakra|1=Śakra (Buddhism)}}. He is brother of Rāhu and his children are all named "Veroca." **Abode: "Double Leisure Castle" ({{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: 雙遊城; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Shuāngyóu-chéng''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|qid=Q192509|lt=Romaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Sōyu-jō'') 8,000 yojanas in height and width, located in a land called "Moon Garland" ({{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: 月鬘; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Yuèmán''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|qid=Q192509|lt=Romaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Getsuman'') 21000 yojanas beneath the second layer of the ocean floor. **Karma: In a past life, he stole others' property. He held wrong views and gave to those following the path of greed, satisfying them with food and drink. As a result of this, he fell into the asura realm. **Lifespan: 600 human years is equal to a day and night in Bali's realm, which lasts for 6,000 years *'''[[Kharakaṇṭha]]''' or ''Kharaskandha'' **Abode: "Gāmbhīra Castle" ({{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: 鋡毘羅城; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Hánpíluó-chéng''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|qid=Q192509|lt=Romaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Kanbira-jō''), 8,000 yojanas in height and width, located in a land called "Sunava" ({{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: 修那婆; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Xiūnàpó''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|qid=Q192509|lt=Romaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Shunaba'') 21000 yojanas beneath the third layer of the ocean floor. **Karma: In a past life, he gave food to those who broke the precepts. On festival days he often misconducted himself in such sports as wrestling and archery. He also gave impure things. As a result of this, he fell into the asura realm. **Lifespan: 700 human years is equal to a day and night in Bali's realm which lasts for 7,000 years. *'''{{ill|Vemacitrin|en|Vemacitrin|qid=Q3538740|lt=Vemacitrin|1=Vemacitrin}}''' (Pāli: ''Vepacitti'') **Bio: He married a gandharva maiden who gave birth to [[:en:Shachi|Shachi]]. Shachi later married Śakra, making Vemacitrin the king of the deva's father-in-law. **Abode: "Gāmbhīra Castle" ({{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: 鋡毘羅城; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Hánpíluó-chéng''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|qid=Q192509|lt=Romaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Kanbira-jō''), 13,000 yojanas in height and width, located in a land called "Unmoving" ({{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: 不動; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Bùdòng''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|qid=Q192509|lt=Romaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Fudō'') 21000 yojanas beneath the third layer of the ocean floor. **Karma: In a past life, he gave to those who kept the precepts but held evil views. Other times he offered protection to trees for his own sake. As a result of this, he fell into the asura realm. According to the {{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|qid=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra|1=Lotus Sutra}}, the four leaders of the asuras took refuge in the Buddha after hearing his sermon. ==Mythological objects== The asuras were said to be in possession of a [[:en:war drum|war drum]] called ''Ālambara'', which sounded like the peal of thunder. It was created from the claw of a giant crab named Kulīradaha. It has since been repurposed by Śakra.{{cite book |last1=Malalasekera |first1=G.P. |title=Dictionary of Pali Proper Names |date=2007 |publisher=Motilal Banarsidass Publishers |isbn=978-81-208-3020-2 |url=http://www.aimwell.org/DPPN/anaka.html |chapter=Ānaka (Āṇaka)|qq=}} Mahayana texts also mention a stringed instrument belonging to the asuras rendered in Chinese as ''Āxiūluó Qín'' ({{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: 阿修羅琴; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Āxiūluó Qín''), literally meaning "asura harp".{{cite book |editor1-last=総合仏教大辞典編集委員会 |title=総合仏教大辞典 |date=1988 |publisher=法蔵館 |page=13 |language=ja|qq=}} {{clear|qq=}} ==See also== *[[:en:Asura (Hinduism)|Asura (Hinduism)]] *{{ill|Ahura|en|Ahura|qid=Q909873|lt=Ahura|1=Ahura}}, divinities in {{ill|Zoroastrianism|en|Zoroastrianism|qid=Q9601|lt=Zoroastrianism|1=Zoroastrianism}} *{{ill|Æsir|en|Æsir|qid=Q170358|lt=Æsir|1=Æsir}} *{{ill|Ashur (god)|en|Ashur (god)|qid=Q685878|lt=Asur|1=Ashur (god)}}, a deity in {{ill|Assyrian mythology|en|Assyrian mythology|qid=Q466340|lt=Assyrian mythology|1=Assyrian mythology}} *{{ill|Jötunn|en|Jötunn|qid=Q210053|lt=Jötunn|1=Jötunn}} in {{ill|Norse mythology|en|Norse mythology|qid=Q128285|lt=Norse mythology|1=Norse mythology}} *{{ill|Nephilim|en|Nephilim|qid=Q747737|lt=Nephilim|1=Nephilim}} in [[:en:Abrahamic mythology|Abrahamic mythology]] *[[:en:Titan (mythology)|Titan (mythology)]] in {{ill|Greek mythology|en|Greek mythology|qid=Q34726|lt=Greek mythology|1=Greek mythology}} * {{ill|Buddhist cosmology|en|Buddhist cosmology|qid=Q1308653|lt=Buddhist cosmology|1=Buddhist cosmology}} * {{ill|Eight Legions|en|Eight Legions|qid=Q1045328|lt=Eight Legions|1=Eight Legions}} ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note|qq=}} ==References== {{reflist|qq=}} {{Buddhism topics|qq=}} [[wiktionary:antigod|antigod]] [[en:Asura (Buddhism)]] [[ar:أسورا (بوذية)]] [[bg:Асура (будизъм)]] [[bn:অসুর (বৌদ্ধধর্ম)]] [[de:Asura (Buddhismus)]] [[es:Asura (budismo)]] [[hu:Aszúra (buddhizmus)]] [[ja:阿修羅]] [[ko:아수라 (불교)]] [[my:အသူရကာယ်]] [[pt:Asura (budismo)]] [[sl:Asure (budizem)]] [[ta:அசுரர் (பௌத்தம்)]] [[wuu:阿修罗（佛教）]] [[zh:阿修罗 (佛教)]] {{wikidata link|Q1637262}} [[Category:Buddhist terminology]] [[Category:Buddhist mythology]] [[Category:Eight heavenly kings]] [[Category:Six realms]] [[Category:天部]] [[Category:阿修羅]]
